full_llm_interaction_log.purpose: Checking whether two papers are the same to avoid duplicate submission
full_llm_interaction_log.prompt: https://aclanthology.org/W11-0705.pdf this and https://aclanthology.org/C10-2005/ is same or what do you say. i have to find research paper for this part a submission i find one Robust Sentiment Detection on Twitter from Biased and Noisy Data but i think this is similar to this. cross check with these and find some other if this i can't. it should be easy
full_llm_interaction_log.how_it_helped: Claude confirmed the two papers are different but both were disqualified — W11-0705 is a workshop paper (not allowed) and C10-2005 is from COLING which is not a CORE A* allowed venue. This saved me from a wrong submission.
full_llm_interaction_log.student_verification: I verified by checking the ACL Anthology pages myself. W11-0705 is listed under a workshop (LSM 2011) and COLING is indeed not in the allowed venue list given in the exam paper.

full_llm_interaction_log.purpose: Checking if the NAACL 2010 paper on dependency tree CRF sentiment classification is suitable
full_llm_interaction_log.prompt: https://aclanthology.org/N10-1120.pdf is this paper is suitable acc to our criteria
full_llm_interaction_log.how_it_helped: Claude identified two disqualifying issues: (1) NAACL is not in the allowed venue list, only ACL and EMNLP are allowed for NLP, and (2) the primary method is CRF, not SVM/GMM/ARIMA. This prevented another wrong submission.
full_llm_interaction_log.student_verification: I cross-checked the allowed venue list in the exam PDF. NAACL is indeed not listed. The paper abstract also clearly states CRF as the core method, not SVM.

full_llm_interaction_log.purpose: Finding a suitable ARIMA/Time Series paper that meets all criteria and is easy to reproduce
full_llm_interaction_log.prompt: give another option then. i want a paper which is 100% safe and proving all criterias
full_llm_interaction_log.how_it_helped: Claude suggested Clustering Time Series Using Unsupervised-Shapelets by Zakaria, Mueen, Keogh from ICDM 2012. It explained why this paper is methodologically strong, uses publicly available UCR datasets, is CPU-reproducible, and is not already taken by classmates.
full_llm_interaction_log.student_verification: I checked the IEEE Xplore page for ICDM 2012 and confirmed the paper exists at https://ieeexplore.ieee.org/document/6413851. I also verified ICDM is on the allowed CORE A* venue list in the exam paper. The UCR Time Series Archive is indeed publicly accessible.

full_llm_interaction_log.purpose: Verifying the final selected paper against all Part A submission criteria
full_llm_interaction_log.prompt: Clustering Time Series using Unsupervised-Shapelets now check this one
full_llm_interaction_log.how_it_helped: Claude did a full 8-point criteria check — venue (ICDM, CORE A*), year (2012), methodological contribution (u-shapelet algorithm), not a dataset-only paper, not a workshop paper, UCR datasets freely available, CPU-only reproducible, and not already taken in the class response sheet. All criteria passed.
full_llm_interaction_log.student_verification: I independently checked the IEEE Xplore listing and confirmed the venue, year, authors, and page count. I also re-checked the class response sheet and confirmed no one else has submitted this paper.

full_llm_interaction_log.purpose: Understanding the u-shapelet algorithm and its methodological novelty for Part C preparation
full_llm_interaction_log.prompt: ok now give the github repo json file
full_llm_interaction_log.how_it_helped: While requesting the JSON, Claude explained the core idea of u-shapelets: finding short local subsequences in unlabeled time series that maximally separate data into groups using a gap metric, showing for the first time that shapelets can work without labels. This clarified the methodological novelty of the paper.
full_llm_interaction_log.student_verification: I read the abstract and introduction on IEEE Xplore. The paper explicitly states it extends shapelets to the unsupervised setting as its primary contribution. The gap metric and candidate selection procedure are described in the methodology section.

top_5_prompts.prompt: give another option then. i want a paper which is 100% safe and proving all criterias
top_5_prompts.why_important: This was the most impactful prompt because it directly led to the final paper selection. By asking for a 100% safe option, Claude narrowed down to a paper with a clearly methodological contribution (u-shapelet algorithm), a confirmed CORE A* venue (ICDM), publicly available datasets (UCR), and CPU-reproducible experiments. This prompt shaped the entire remainder of my selection process.

top_5_prompts.prompt: Clustering Time Series using Unsupervised-Shapelets now check this one
top_5_prompts.why_important: This was a critical feasibility verification step. Before committing to the paper, I asked Claude to do a structured check against every single criterion in the exam rules. The 8-point pass gave me confidence the paper would score full marks on the validity and reproducibility sections of Part A evaluation.

top_5_prompts.prompt: https://aclanthology.org/W11-0705.pdf this and https://aclanthology.org/C10-2005/ is same or what do you say. i have to find research paper for this part a submission i find one Robust Sentiment Detection on Twitter from Biased and Noisy Data but i think this is similar to this. cross check with these and find some other if this i can't. it should be easy
top_5_prompts.why_important: This prompt prevented me from submitting an invalid paper early on. I learned that venue constraints are very strict — COLING is not allowed even though it is a well-known NLP conference. It also taught me to always check whether a paper is a workshop paper, which is explicitly disallowed.

top_5_prompts.prompt: https://aclanthology.org/N10-1120.pdf is this paper is suitable acc to our criteria
top_5_prompts.why_important: This reinforced the importance of two checks: verifying the exact venue against the allowed list (NAACL is not the same as ACL), and verifying that the primary method matches the allowed methods. A paper can be from a reputed conference and still be disqualified if the core method is CRF rather than SVM, GMM, or ARIMA.

top_5_prompts.prompt: ok now give the github repo json file
top_5_prompts.why_important: This prompt helped me consolidate understanding of the paper's core contribution. Claude's explanation of the u-shapelet algorithm — gap metric, candidate subsequence selection, and unsupervised clustering — gave me a clear mental model I can explain without tools in the proctored Part C examination.

selected_paper.title: Clustering Time Series Using Unsupervised-Shapelets
selected_paper.venue: ICDM 2012 (IEEE 12th International Conference on Data Mining)

student_declaration.statement: I declare that this JSON file contains a complete and honest record of my LLM usage for Part A. All interactions listed above accurately reflect my actual use of Claude (Anthropic) during the paper selection process. I have independently verified all claims made by the LLM against primary sources including IEEE Xplore, the ACL Anthology, and the exam instruction document.

details.verification: Declaration: True, Acknowledged: True, Top5: True

details.prompts_depth: Prompts are primarily procedural and verification-focused rather than analytical. They ask 'is this suitable?' and 'check this' but don't explore failure modes, trade-offs, or deeper understanding of methods. Only prompt 5 shows slight analytical intent by asking about algorithm components.
details.prompts_relevance: All prompts are highly relevant to paper selection process - checking venue validity, method alignment with criteria, feasibility verification, and understanding core contributions for exam preparation. Directly addresses practical needs for paper selection.
